sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 393, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at (27,36), 16px. 'N' at (24,36). Player adjacent to 'b' frame (left) and 'c' frame (right). 'P' bar at 3.
- Plan: Try SPACE - player is between both frames, might absorb one.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 392 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 5.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so SPACE from this position created a 'G' frame at (28, 36) with 12 pixels. The player 'W' is back to 4 pixels at (27, 36). The 'b' frame is still at (28, 28) with 18 pixels, and the 'c' frame is still at (28, 40) with 24 pixels.
So SPACE from this position didn't absorb a frame, it created a new 'G' frame at (28, 36). This is different from before.
Let me think about what's happening. The player is at (27, 36), and the 'N' object is at (24, 36). The 'G' frame appeared at (28, 36), which is between the player and the bottom area.
Wait, looking at the ascii:
Row 27: wwwwwwwwwwwwWWWWwwwwwwwwwwwww
Row 28: wwwwbbbbbbbbGGGGccccccccwwwww
Row 29: wwwwbgggggggGbbGcbbccbbcwwwww
Row 30: wwwwbgggggggGbbGcbbccbbcwwwww
Row 31: wwwwbbbbbbbbGGGGccccccccwwwww
The 'G' frame is at (28, 36) with 12 pixels. It's a 4x4 frame. The player 'W' is at (27, 36) with 4 pixels.
So SPACE created a 'G' frame below the player. This is different from absorbing a frame.
Let me think about the pattern:
1. When the player was at (28, 39) and pressed SPACE, it absorbed the 'G' frame at (28, 40) (to the right).
2. When the player was at (31, 36) and pressed SPACE, it absorbed the 'G' frame at (32, 36) (below).
3. When the player was at (27, 36) and pressed SPACE, it created a 'G' frame at (28, 36) (below).
So SPACE seems to interact with the area below or to the right of the player. When there's a frame there, it absorbs it. When there's no frame, it creates one.
But wait, in case 1, the player was at (28, 39) and the 'G' frame was at (28, 40) to the right. In case 2, the player was at (31, 36) and the 'G' frame was at (32, 36) below. In case 3, the player was at (27, 36) and there was no frame below, so it created one.
Hmm, but in case 3, the player is at (27, 36), and below it is row 28, cols 36-39, which is where the 'G' frame appeared. So SPACE creates a frame below the player if there isn't one, or absorbs a frame below the player if there is one.
But wait, in case 1, the player was at (28, 39), and the 'G' frame was at (28, 40) to the right, not below. So maybe SPACE interacts with the frame that's adjacent to the player, either below or to the right.
Let me reconsider. The player is a 4x4 block. When the player is at (28, 39), it spans rows 28-31, cols 39-42 (or something like that). The 'G' frame at (28, 40) is to the right of the player.
Actually, I need to be more careful about the player's position. The segmentation says the player 'W' is at (27, 36) with 4 pixels. But looking at the ascii, the player is at row 27, cols 36-39 (4 pixels). So the player is a 1x4 horizontal bar, not a 4x4 block.
Wait, but earlier when the player absorbed the bottom 'G' frame, the player became 16 pixels, which is a 4x4 block. Let me check the ascii again:
Row 27: wwwwwwwwwwwwWWWWwwwwwwwwwwwww
Row 28: wwwwbbbbbbbbGGGGccccccccwwwww
Row 29: wwwwbgggggggGbbGcbbccbbcwwwww
Row 30: wwwwbgggggggGbbGcbbccbbcwwwww
Row 31: wwwwbbbbbbbbGGGGccccccccwwwww
The player 'W' is only at row 27, cols 36-39 (4 pixels). So the player is back to a 1x4 horizontal bar.
So SPACE toggles the player between 4 pixels (1x4 bar) and 16 pixels (4x4 block). When the player is 16 pixels, it absorbs the adjacent frame. When the player is 4 pixels, SPACE creates a frame below the player.
Wait, but earlier when the player was at (28, 39) with 4 pixels and pressed SPACE, it became 16 pixels and absorbed the 'G' frame. Then when the player was at (28, 39) with 16 pixels and pressed SPACE again, it became 4 pixels and the 'G' frame reappeared.
So SPACE toggles: 4px -> 16px (absorb frame) -> 4px (release frame).
But in this case, the player was at (27, 36) with 4 pixels, and SPACE created a 'G' frame at (28, 36). The player stayed at 4 pixels.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me think about it differently.
Maybe SPACE doesn't toggle the player's size, but rather creates or absorbs a frame at a specific location relative to the player. The player's size changes as a side effect of absorbing or releasing a frame.
Let me try SPACE again to see what happens. If the player is at (27, 36) with 4 pixels, and there's a 'G' frame at (28, 36), maybe SPACE will absorb the 'G' frame.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at (27,36), 4px. 'G' frame at (28,36), 12px. 'N' at (24,36). 'b' frame (18px) and 'c' frame (24px) remain. 'P' bar at 3.
Plan: Try SPACE again - might absorb the newly created 'G' frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Try SPACE again
result = action(['SPACE'])
print(f"SPACE result: gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}, done={last_action_result.get('done')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nKey objects:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] <= 50:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min = min(r for r,c in b)
        c_min = min(c for r,c in b)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pos=({r_min},{c_min}), pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check ascii
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nAscii rows 24-38, cols 24-52:")
for r in range(24, 39):
    print(f"Row {r}: {lines[r][24:53]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
